refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) matching customer deployment scenarios. Measure end-to-end loss across the teleoperation link (operator input → vehicle command execution → telemetry return). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit for ≥95% of test runs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time packet loss visibility to the operator and safety manager.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or halt operation if packet loss exceeds the customer-defined safety threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive bitrate encoding and packet prioritization in the teleoperation link to reduce payload size and prioritize critical commands over telemetry.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication paths (dual network interfaces, mesh relay nodes) to increase path diversity and reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-C: Implement forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission of critical packets to recover from transient loss.
DP-D: Combine DP-A, DP-B, and DP-C with real-time monitoring and operator-facing packet loss metrics to enable informed operational decisions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered teleoperation communication strategy combining adaptive bitrate encoding with packet prioritization (DP-A), redundant communication paths with automatic failover (DP-B), forward error correction and selective retransmission (DP-C), and real-time packet loss monitoring with operator/safety manager visibility.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation link design (network layer) with vehicle command/telemetry processing (application layer) and operator interface (HMI layer). This is acceptable because packet loss is inherently a cross-layer concern.
DP-D couples safety monitoring (SafetyAlert interface) with communication quality (Network Link interface). This is necessary and well-justified by high safety criticality.
coupling_check.notes: No problematic decoupling identified. The multi-layered approach is appropriate for a safety-critical, quality-of-service requirement. Coupling is intentional and justified.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer will define and agree on network conditions (e.g., WiFi 5GHz in open field, 4G LTE in urban canyon, hybrid mesh) before acceptance testing.
Assumption: Vehicle and teleoperation subsystems have sufficient computational headroom to support FEC encoding/decoding and adaptive bitrate logic without introducing latency.
Risk: Redundant communication paths increase system complexity, cost, and power consumption. Trade-off analysis required.
Risk: FEC overhead may increase latency if not carefully tuned; latency itself is a safety concern in teleoperation.
Risk: Operator/safety manager may misinterpret packet loss metrics or over-rely on them, leading to unsafe operational decisions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) packet prioritization rules (e.g., emergency stop commands always sent with FEC, telemetry sent with adaptive bitrate); (2) latency budget for FEC/retransmission (e.g., max 200ms round-trip); (3) operator alert thresholds (e.g., warn at 0.5% loss, halt at 1.5% loss); (4) network condition profiles to be tested (WiFi, cellular, hybrid, mesh).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication link between the operator (or tele-operator) and the vehicle, including adaptive bitrate encoding, packet prioritization, redundant path management, forward error correction, selective retransmission, and real-time packet loss monitoring. The module interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking and Telemetry) and with Safety Services (via SafetyAlert and Network Link status).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation sends operator commands (steering, throttle, bucket control, etc.) to Vehicle. Tasking packets are prioritized and may be protected with FEC. Latency budget: ≤200ms round-trip.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation receives vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, diagnostics) from Vehicle. Telemetry is subject to adaptive bitrate encoding based on network conditions. Frequency: ≥10 Hz for safety-critical sensors, ≥1 Hz for non-critical.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation reports packet loss metrics and network health to Safety Services. Safety Services may command Teleoperation to halt or degrade operation if packet loss exceeds threshold. Latency: ≤100ms.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation manages the physical/logical network link(s) (WiFi, cellular, mesh, hybrid). Network Link provides real-time link quality metrics (RSSI, SNR, latency, loss rate) to Teleoperation. Teleoperation configures redundancy and failover policies.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse packet loss logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and Network Link modules. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle owns command execution and telemetry generation, but not the communication link itself. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would require Vehicle to manage operator-side encoding, redundant paths, and operator-facing metrics—poor separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Teleoperation is the natural owner of the teleoperation link, including all communication quality mechanisms (adaptive bitrate, FEC, redundancy, monitoring). All DP-D logic is contained within Teleoperation's scope. Interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link (link management). Highly cohesive, minimal diffusion. Strongly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, but not the communication link itself. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would blur the boundary between communication quality and safety policy. Safety Services should consume packet loss metrics from Teleoperation and decide on operational halts. Not recommended as primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D because: (1) it encapsulates all communication quality mechanisms (adaptive bitrate, FEC, redundancy, monitoring) with minimal diffusion; (2) it has clear, well-defined interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link (link status); (3) it maintains separation of concerns—Teleoperation handles 'how to communicate reliably,' Safety Services handles 'when to halt based on communication quality'; (4) it aligns with industry practice (e.g., ROS middleware, automotive V2X stacks).
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if it must support multiple network types (WiFi, cellular, mesh, hybrid) with different FEC/redundancy strategies. Mitigation: Use a pluggable network abstraction layer (Network Link interface) to isolate network-specific logic.

Risk: Operator-facing packet loss metrics (part of DP-D) may require HMI integration. If HMI is owned by a different module, this creates a dependency. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface to carry packet loss metrics; HMI module consumes SafetyAlert.

Risk: Real-time monitoring and adaptive bitrate logic may introduce latency or CPU overhead. Mitigation: Conduct performance analysis during DP refinement; set latency budget (e.g., ≤50ms for adaptive bitrate decision).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has ownership of: (1) operator-side command encoding and transmission; (2) vehicle-side telemetry reception and decoding; (3) redundant path management and failover logic; (4) FEC/retransmission state machines; (5) packet loss metrics collection and reporting. Clarify that Network Link module provides only low-level link management (interface up/down, RSSI, SNR); Teleoperation owns higher-level redundancy and failover decisions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D directly addresses the refined FR by implementing multiple mechanisms to achieve and maintain packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold. DP-D is complete (covers encoding, redundancy, error correction, monitoring, and operator visibility) and measurable (packet loss can be quantified and compared to acceptance criterion). No decoupling or missing logic identified.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: Teleoperation module contains all DP-D logic with minimal spillover. Vehicle module receives Tasking commands and sends Telemetry but does not implement communication quality mechanisms. Safety Services consumes packet loss metrics but does not implement FEC or redundancy. Network Link provides link status but does not implement adaptive bitrate or failover.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation achieves 0.95 containment score. The 0.05 diffusion is acceptable and represents necessary cross-module interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No problematic diffusion identified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: packet loss can be measured end-to-end using standard network tools (tcpdump, iperf, custom telemetry loggers). Acceptance testing can be conducted in controlled lab environments (WiFi, cellular emulation) and live field trials. Operator/safety manager visibility can be validated via HMI inspection and user acceptance testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns DP-D implementation; Vehicle module provides Tasking/Telemetry interfaces; Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert; Network Link provides link status. Responsibilities are well-defined and testable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify packet prioritization rules (e.g., emergency stop always FEC-protected, telemetry adaptive bitrate).
Define latency budget for FEC/retransmission (e.g., max 200ms round-trip) to ensure safety-critical responsiveness.
Specify operator alert thresholds (e.g., warn at 0.5% loss, halt at 1.5% loss) and tie them to customer-defined acceptance criterion.
Confirm network condition profiles to be tested (WiFi, cellular, hybrid, mesh) and document in acceptance test plan.
Clarify ownership boundary between Teleoperation (communication quality) and Safety Services (safety policy enforcement) to avoid overlap.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed and non-atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (error detection/recovery, operator visibility, graceful degradation). DP-D is sound and addresses the FR completely. Teleoperation module is the optimal owner with high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). All gates pass. However, DP-D itself is complex and multi-layered (adaptive bitrate, FEC, redundancy, monitoring); further decomposition is necessary to allocate design decisions to specific sub-modules or design elements within Teleoperation. Recommend decomposing DP-D into lower-level DPs for each sub-FR and then assigning to Teleoperation sub-components (e.g., Adaptive Bitrate Engine, FEC Codec, Redundancy Manager, Monitoring Agent).
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time packet loss visibility to the operator and safety manager.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or halt operation if packet loss exceeds the customer-defined safety threshold.

assumptions: Customer will define and agree on network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid, mesh) before acceptance testing.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems have sufficient computational headroom to support FEC and adaptive bitrate logic without introducing unacceptable latency (budget: ≤200ms round-trip).
Operator and safety manager will be trained to interpret packet loss metrics and make informed operational decisions.
Network Link module provides low-level link status (RSSI, SNR, latency, loss rate); Teleoperation owns higher-level redundancy and failover logic.
SafetyAlert interface can carry packet loss metrics and operational halt commands between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
open_risks: Redundant communication paths increase system complexity, cost, and power consumption. Trade-off analysis required during DP refinement.
FEC overhead may increase latency if not carefully tuned. Latency itself is a safety concern in teleoperation. Mitigation: set and validate latency budget.
Operator/safety manager may misinterpret packet loss metrics or over-rely on them, leading to unsafe operational decisions. Mitigation: provide clear HMI design and operator training.
Network condition variability (WiFi interference, cellular handover, mesh routing changes) may cause packet loss to fluctuate. Mitigation: implement adaptive thresholds and hysteresis in halt logic.

Acceptance criterion is customer-defined (≤1% or other target). If customer does not define target, acceptance testing cannot proceed. Mitigation: establish customer agreement on network conditions and packet loss limit before design finalization.
evidence_needed: Customer agreement on network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid, mesh) and packet loss acceptance criterion (e.g., ≤1%).
Latency budget analysis: confirm that FEC encoding/decoding and adaptive bitrate logic do not exceed ≤200ms round-trip budget.
Performance analysis: measure CPU/memory overhead of FEC, redundancy management, and monitoring on Vehicle and Teleoperation platforms.
Network simulation results: packet loss measurements under various network conditions (WiFi 5GHz, 4G LTE, mesh relay, hybrid) with DP-D mechanisms enabled.
Operator/safety manager feedback: HMI mockup review and user acceptance testing of packet loss metrics and alert thresholds.
Safety analysis: FMEA or HARA to identify failure modes of communication quality mechanisms and their impact on safety.
Interface specification: detailed Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interface definitions (message formats, latency, reliability).
